summary: chai typosquat fetches and executes remote JavaScript on require()
details: Package impersonates the legitimate `chai` assertion library (copied description, README, and contributor list; homepage points to chaiplugin.com rather than chaijs.com). On `require('chai-plugin-helper')`, index.js spawns a detached Node child process targeting lib/chai/utils/addAssertion.js: `const chaiBinding = spawn("node", [addAssertion, JSON.stringify(args)], { detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });`. addAssertion.js is obfuscator.io-encoded (string-array rotation with a custom base64 alphabet decoder) hiding the strings `https`, `data`, `end`, `require`, plus the destination host. The decoded flow performs an HTTPS GET, collects the response body, then runs `new Function('require', body)(require)` — executing attacker-controlled JavaScript inside the consumer's Node process with full `require` access (filesystem, network, child_process). The detached child + ignored stdio makes the exec silent. The combination of identity impersonation, heavy obfuscation hiding the destination URL, and require-time fetch-and-eval is the canonical supply-chain dropper pattern.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
